title: How digital infrastructure is strengthening palliative care delivery in Kerala
summary: Bodhish Thomas, Founder, Open Healthcare Network Foundation, explains how Kerala Care and open-source digital infrastructure are strengthening palliative care delivery through coordinated, community-led healthcare
thbAction: Map the news to THB's provider CRM for referral growth, patient lifecycle engagement, and multi-facility relationship management across hospital and provider networks.
contentHtml:
Bodhish Thomas, Founder, Open Healthcare Network Foundation, explains how Kerala Care and open-source digital infrastructure are strengthening palliative care delivery through coordinated, community-led healthcare

In most places, palliative care is imagined as a hospital service delivered near the end of life for cancer patients. Kerala built a different idea over three decades: care as a neighbourhood responsibility. Volunteers, ASHA workers, nurses, doctors, local governments and community organisations have together made home-based support part of the state’s public health imagination.

The new Kerala story is that this human network is now being strengthened by public digital infrastructure. Kerala Care is a live palliative-care grid, powered by CARE, the open-source Digital Public Good. stewarded by Open Healthcare Network Foundation.

That distinction matters. A Digital Public Good is not just code. It is infrastructure that governments can own, adapt and scale without being locked into a vendor’s roadmap. CARE has been built in the open by 1,300+ open-source contributors across its ecosystem. It is reusable, standards-oriented and designed for real health-system workflows: patient registries, home visits, care-team coordination, follow-ups, referrals, dashboards, audit trails and programme visibility.

Before a grid, continuity depended on people holding the system together manually. A nurse knew which patient needed dressing. A volunteer remembered which family required medicine support. A panchayat team kept paper registers. WhatsApp groups carried urgent messages. These methods reflected deep commitment, but they also made the system fragile. When care lives only in notebooks, phones and individual memory, the state cannot reliably see who is covered, who is overdue, which referral is pending, or where suffering is accumulating.

Kerala Care changes this by turning compassion into a coordinated operating system. Today, the grid connects 1,400+ government facilities and 1,305+ NGOs and community-based organisations. It has registered 340,826 patients and recorded 1.68 million home visits in the last 12 months. Behind every number is something profoundly human: a patient seen at home, a task completed, a follow-up created, a family made visible to the system.

At a time when digital health is often announced before it is operational, Kerala Care is visible, functioning and accountable. The public dashboard moves palliative care from anecdote to evidence. Citizens, administrators and care teams can see that the grid is not a promise in a policy document; it is working in the field. Live visibility makes it harder for invisible patients to remain invisible. It also makes it easier for districts to learn, compare, improve and close gaps.

This is the real promise of digital transformation in healthcare. It is not digitisation for its own sake. It is continuity. When a volunteer visits a home, the system remembers. When a patient’s condition worsens, escalation is possible. When a district falls behind, the gap can be seen. When another state wants to learn from Kerala, it does not have to start from a blank page.

For India, this matters urgently. Ageing, cancer, non-communicable diseases, disability, migration and smaller families are shifting care from the hospital ward to the home. Hospitals alone cannot carry this burden. Families alone should not be left to carry it either.

Kerala Care shows a path forward: community strength, government stewardship and open digital infrastructure working together. CARE’s role in this story is proof that a Digital Public Good built by a community can move beyond repositories, demos and conferences to become the backbone of live public service delivery.

Kerala has already shown India that palliative care can be compassionate. It is now showing that compassion can be organised, measured and scaled without losing its human core.
